query: What do MESSENGER MDIS images show about the relationship between Mercury's north polar low reflectance deposits and permanently shadowed terrain?

row.paper: Chabot, N.L., et al., 2016. Imaging Mercury's polar deposits during MESSENGER's low-altitude campaign. GRL, 43(18), pp.9461-9468. DOI:10.1002/2016GL070403
row.query: What do MESSENGER MDIS images show about the relationship between Mercury's north polar low reflectance deposits and permanently shadowed terrain?

tool_input.summary: Located MESSENGER MDIS PDS3 archives on the IMG node. For studying north polar low-reflectance deposits (LRD) versus permanently shadowed terrain (PST/PSR), the most directly useful products are the MDIS map-projected basemaps and high-incidence mosaics (to outline shadowed crater interiors) plus multispectral/low-incidence mosaics (to quantify reflectance). IMG appears to host the PDS3 volumes (not the newer PDS4 MDIS archive, which is typically at the Geosciences node).

candidates.title: MESS MDIS HIGH-INCIDENCE BASEMAP ILLUM FROM EAST DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1 (directory walk + probe): High-incidence (low Sun) polar basemap mosaic illuminated from the east—this is the MDIS product most commonly used to map the boundaries of permanently shadowed terrain and to compare those boundaries with adjacent low-reflectance deposits at Mercury’s north pole.

candidates.title: MESS MDIS HIGH-INCIDENCE BASEMAP ILLUM FROM WEST DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Companion high-incidence polar basemap illuminated from the west. Using both east- and west-lit mosaics helps distinguish true low-reflectance surface materials from illumination effects and better delineate permanently shadowed crater floors/walls.

candidates.title: MESS MDIS MAP PROJECTED LOW-INCIDENCE ANGLE BASEMAP DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Low-incidence (more ‘normal’ lighting) MDIS basemap mosaic—useful for measuring/confirming the low reflectance of LRD outside of deep shadow and for correlating LRD distribution with mapped PSR locations derived from topography/illumination modeling.

candidates.title: MESSENGER MDIS MAP PROJECTED MULTISPECTRAL DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Map-projected MDIS multispectral mosaics enable color/relative reflectance comparisons of LRD versus surrounding terrain, while still being co-registered for comparison to PSR outlines (from shadow maps or DEM-based illumination models).

candidates.title: MESSENGER MDIS CALIBRATED DATA
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1: Calibrated individual MDIS images (CDRs). If you want to reproduce the published LRD–PSR comparisons directly (rather than relying on mosaics), this archive provides the calibrated inputs for custom polar projections, photometric normalization, and reflectance analysis.

candidates.title: MESSENGER MDIS DIGITAL ELEVATION MODEL ARCHIVE
candidates.reasoning: Stage 1 (resolve + probe): While not an image mosaic, the MDIS DEM is commonly paired with MDIS imaging to model permanent shadow at the north pole (terrain-controlled illumination) and then compare predicted PSR extent with observed low-reflectance deposits in MDIS basemaps.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS JPL Imaging Node (IMG) node at https://planetarydata.jpl.nasa.gov/

Directory layout and workflow:
Directory layout: img/data/<mission>/[<sub-tree>/]<dataset_or_bundle>/
Top level under img/data/ is a flat list of mission directories. Many missions nest one or
two more levels before reaching dataset roots (Cassini is the most extreme — four parallel
sub-trees). Recurse with list_dataset_dirs rather than guessing paths.
There is no holdings/inventory page — the Apache directory listing is the only index.
PDS4 coverage at IMG is partial: for some missions the legacy PDS3 volumes are mirrored
here but the equivalent urn:nasa:pds:<mission>_<inst>_* PDS4 bundles live at a different
discipline node. If a urn: LID does not turn up after one list+probe, return the closest
PDS3 DATA_SET_ID you found while probing and note that IMG hosts the PDS3 mirror only.


Naming conventions on IMG:
  Top level: lowercase mission directories (cassini/, galileo/, mariner9/, viking_orbiter/, …).
  Inside a mission: variable structure. Cassini for example has cassini_orbiter/, opus/, pds4/, and public/ — all four can contain datasets at different depths.
  PDS3 dataset names: hyphenated identifiers (e.g. co-s-iss-2-edr-v1.0); PDS4 bundles begin with urn-nasa-pds-.
Skip these directories at every level when scanning: checksums, document, index, catalog,
extras, browse, software, errata.
Volume-numbering convention (many IMG missions follow this pattern):
  Within a single mission/instrument directory, numbered sibling volumes encode the
  product level (raw → calibrated → derived) in the leading digit(s) of their name.
  Examples of the pattern (NOT a recipe for any one query):
    MRO CTX: img/data/mro/ctx/mrox_NNNN/   — flat EDR series, all the same DATA_SET_ID.
    MESSENGER MDIS: img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_<lvl><nnn>/  — leading digit splits
      product levels (lower digit = lower processing level; higher digit = derived).
  Use pds_resolve_volume with the relevant level keyword (EDR / CDR / RDR / BDR / MDR /
  derived / calibrated) as the dataset_id_hint to land on the volume matching the query,
  rather than probing many sibling volumes individually.


PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

YOUR TOOLS (5 tools — always pass node='img'):
  1. pds_list_missions(node='img') — returns mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP.
  2. pds_list_dataset_dirs(path, node='img', filter=...) — lists sub-directory names under a path. Cheap HTTP, no label parsing.
  3. pds_probe_datasets(paths, node='img') — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Accepts a list of paths (max 20).
  4. pds_inspect_collections(path, node='img') — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection labels.
  5. pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path, node='img', dataset_id_hint=..., sample=8) — when a parent directory contains many
     numbered sibling volumes (RMS COISS_2xxx, COUVIS_0xxx, ATM
     MROM_*, jnomwr_*, cocirs_*, IMG mrox_*, MSGRMDS_*), this probes
     hint-ranked children in one call and returns per-child
     dataset_ids plus a `best_match` field. Use it instead of
     probing volumes one at a time when you know the target id or
     a discriminating substring of it.

WORKFLOW:
Step 1: If you know the mission directory from the abbreviation table, skip directly to list_dataset_dirs(path='img/data/<mission>/', node='img'). Otherwise call pds_list_missions(node='img') first to see the mission list.
Step 2: Call pds_list_dataset_dirs for the mission directory. If results look like another layer of organisational sub-trees (e.g. cassini_orbiter/, opus/, pds4/, public/ for Cassini, or ctx/, hirise/, marci/ for mro), call list_dataset_dirs again on the relevant sub-tree.
Step 3: Volume-set targets — when a dataset is split across many numbered volumes inside a
single parent directory, call pds_resolve_volume(volume_set_path='<parent path>/', node='img', dataset_id_hint='<DATA_SET_ID fragment>', sample=8). It returns per-child dataset_ids in one call instead of multiple sequential probes.
Step 4: If PDS4 bundles are found, call pds_inspect_collections on top 2-3.
Step 5: COVERAGE-GAP rule. If the target is a urn:nasa:pds: LID and no matching bundle turns up after Steps 1–4, OR if listing the mission/instrument directory returns only
ancillary content (EXTRAS/, browse/, document/) with no voldesc.cat in any probed sub-
directory, then IMG most likely does not mirror that archive — many instruments have
their main archive at a different discipline node (per the mission notes: HiRISE main
RDR → GEO, MESSENGER MDIS PDS4 → PDS-Geosciences, etc.). Return the closest available
PDS3 DATA_SET_ID (synthesised from the abbreviation table if no probe has succeeded
yet) and note in `reasoning` that the main archive lives at the other node. Do not
burn more than 2 extra list/probe calls hunting for the missing form.
Step 6: Use the `dataset_ids` field on pds_probe_datasets results when a voldesc ships multiple DATA_SET_IDs — match against the full list, not just the scalar.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires at least one candidate. Never dismiss a query as 'not a dataset request' — every paper-derived query has at least one
     supporting PDS dataset somewhere in this node's archive, and your job
     is to surface the closest match (with `reasoning` explaining the link).
  2. Prefer CALIBRATED/REDUCED data over raw/EDR for science queries.
     When a probed voldesc exposes `dataset_ids` with multiple entries,
     match the question's data need to the PRODUCT-TYPE token inside each
     id (typically the third or fourth dash-separated token). Common ones:
         EDR / RAW     = raw observations
         CDR / CALIB   = generic calibrated data
         RDR / TRDR / MRDR / MTRDR / BDR / SCVDR = reduced / derived imagery or spectra
         DDR / LDR     = derived / digital terrain / level-5 products
         SSB           = solar / stellar occultation (Cassini UVIS)
         SPEC / CUBE / WAV = spectra / spectral cubes / waveforms
         QUBE          = VIMS spectral data cube (the VIMS-flavoured "EDR")
     If the query mentions 'occultation' return the SSB id; 'spectra' →
     SPEC; 'calibrated radiance' → CALIB/CDR; 'mosaic / map / derived' →
     DDR/MRDR/MTRDR. When two product types both fit the question (e.g.
     SPEC + SSB for an occultation spectra query), return both candidates.
     Do NOT just return the scalar `dataset_id` and drop the rest — that
     field is only the first id in the voldesc and is often the wrong
     product type for a given query.
  3. For PDS4, use inspect_collections ONLY for top 2-3 bundles. Return COLLECTION-level LID when :data or :calibrated exists.
  4. When the SAME data is available as both PDS3 and PDS4, return BOTH identifiers — emit one candidate per identifier. Most published papers cite the PDS3 dataset_id (e.g. MESS-E_V_H_SW-MAG-3-CDR-CALIBRATED-V1.0, LRO-L-LROC-5-RDR-V1.0); the PDS4 collection LID is the modern equivalent. Do NOT silently drop the PDS3 form.
  5. Stay under 20 tool calls per query. Soft cap: 15. Hard cap: 20.
  6. Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  7. ANTI-THRASHING. Never re-issue a tool call with the same name and the
     same key arguments (path, paths, filter) that you've already issued in
     this trace. The result will be identical — it wastes the budget and
     blocks progress. If you're tempted to re-probe the same set of paths,
     either move to a different tool (inspect_collections, resolve_volume)
     or commit to `final_result` with the best candidate you have so far.
  8. STOP-AT-LEVEL. The output `path` field should point at a dataset or
     bundle directory (containing voldesc.cat or bundle*.xml), NOT at a
     deeper product / DATA / EXTRAS / BROWSE / SHAPEFILE / DTM sub-folder.
     Feature-level retrieval (a specific image of a specific crater) is
     out of scope for this finder; return the dataset and explain in
     `reasoning` how it covers the feature.
  9. After 15 tool calls without converging, ALWAYS prefer emitting your
     current best candidate(s) over running another exploratory call.
     A grounded near-match with a clear reasoning note is more useful
     than a 20-call trace that times out with no `final_result`.

===========================================================
STAGE 2 — DEEPER NODE-SPECIFIC SEARCH (escalation only)
===========================================================

Everything above is STAGE 1: the 5 live-HTTP tools (pds_list_missions / pds_list_dataset_dirs / pds_probe_datasets / pds_inspect_collections / pds_resolve_volume), the per-node workflow, and the Critical Rules ALL belong to Stage 1. Stage 1 reaches bundle/collection level by walking the node's directory tree. Stage 2 below is a SECOND set of tools for refining or rescuing Stage 1 — do NOT call any Stage 2 tool unless one of the escalation triggers below fires.

WHEN TO ESCALATE TO STAGE 2 (any one is enough):
  (a) The query asks for SPECIFIC observations, granules, or product IDs — not just a dataset. Example: 'Cassini ISS images of Mimas between 2009 and 2010' needs per-observation filtering.
  (b) Stage 1 has located the right bundle/collection but you still need to narrow by GEOMETRY, TIME, or a SPATIAL filter (bbox, lat/lon, ring longitude, target body, observation type).
  (c) Stage 1 produced AMBIGUOUS candidates that a faceted query can disambiguate (e.g. multiple instruments with similar names, multiple processing levels).
  (d) Stage 1 cannot reach the node's holdings index (e.g. SBN returns HTTP 403, a directory listing is unavailable). Use the Stage 2 API as a fallback path to candidates.

STAGE 2 TOOLS (this node):
  - img_search_tool(target=..., instrument=..., mission=..., time_range=..., filters=...): IMG faceted product search.
  - img_get_facets_tool(field=...): enumerate valid facet values (instruments, missions, targets, product types) for the IMG catalog.
  - img_get_product_tool(product_id=...): fetch metadata for one IMG product.
  - img_count_tool(target=..., instrument=..., ...): count matches without pulling rows.

STAGE 2 RULES:
  1. When you already have a bundle/collection LID from Stage 1, REUSE it as a filter in the Stage 2 call. Stage 2 is refinement, not a fresh search.
  2. Spend the MINIMUM number of additional calls. If a single faceted call answers the question, stop.
  3. If Stage 2 finds nothing or errors out, FALL BACK to the Stage 1 candidates and note in `reasoning` that Stage 2 was attempted and returned no refinement.
  4. The 20-tool-call budget from Stage 1's Critical Rule 5 is the budget for BOTH stages combined. Do NOT reset the counter when entering Stage 2.
  5. Every candidate's `reasoning` field must explicitly name which stage produced it (e.g. 'Stage 1: located bundle X by directory walk' or 'Stage 2: narrowed to product Y via ODE_search_products bbox filter').
What do MESSENGER MDIS images show about the relationship between Mercury's north polar low reflectance deposits and permanently shadowed terrain?

results.title: MESS MDIS MAP PROJECTED LOW-INCIDENCE ANGLE BASEMAP DATA

VOLUME.VOLUME_SET_NAME: MESS MDIS MAP PROJECTED LOW-INCIDENCE ANGLE BASEMAP DATA
VOLUME.VOLUME_NAME: MESS MDIS MAP PROJECTED LOW-INCIDENCE ANGLE BASEMAP ARCHIVE
VOLUME.DESCRIPTION: Low solar incidence angle basemap reduced data records for the MDIS camera system on MESSENGER.

parts.args: {"summary":"Located MESSENGER MDIS PDS3 archives on the IMG node. For studying north polar low-reflectance deposits (LRD) versus permanently shadowed terrain (PST/PSR), the most directly useful products are the MDIS map-projected basemaps and high-incidence mosaics (to outline shadowed crater interiors) plus multispectral/low-incidence mosaics (to quantify reflectance). IMG appears to host the PDS3 volumes (not the newer PDS4 MDIS archive, which is typically at the Geosciences node).","candidates":[{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_7001/","dataset_id":"MESS-H-MDIS-5-RDR-HIE-V1.0","title":"MESS MDIS HIGH-INCIDENCE BASEMAP ILLUM FROM EAST DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1 (directory walk + probe): High-incidence (low Sun) polar basemap mosaic illuminated from the east—this is the MDIS product most commonly used to map the boundaries of permanently shadowed terrain and to compare those boundaries with adjacent low-reflectance deposits at Mercury’s north pole."},{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_7101/","dataset_id":"MESS-H-MDIS-5-RDR-HIW-V1.0","title":"MESS MDIS HIGH-INCIDENCE BASEMAP ILLUM FROM WEST DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1: Companion high-incidence polar basemap illuminated from the west. Using both east- and west-lit mosaics helps distinguish true low-reflectance surface materials from illumination effects and better delineate permanently shadowed crater floors/walls."},{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_7201/","dataset_id":"MESS-H-MDIS-5-RDR-LOI-V1.0","title":"MESS MDIS MAP PROJECTED LOW-INCIDENCE ANGLE BASEMAP DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1: Low-incidence (more ‘normal’ lighting) MDIS basemap mosaic—useful for measuring/confirming the low reflectance of LRD outside of deep shadow and for correlating LRD distribution with mapped PSR locations derived from topography/illumination modeling."},{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_5001/","dataset_id":"MESS-H-MDIS-5-RDR-MDR-V1.0","title":"MESSENGER MDIS MAP PROJECTED MULTISPECTRAL DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1: Map-projected MDIS multispectral mosaics enable color/relative reflectance comparisons of LRD versus surrounding terrain, while still being co-registered for comparison to PSR outlines (from shadow maps or DEM-based illumination models)."},{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MSGRMDS_2001/","dataset_id":"MESS-E/V/H-MDIS-4-CDR-CALDATA-V1.0","title":"MESSENGER MDIS CALIBRATED DATA","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1: Calibrated individual MDIS images (CDRs). If you want to reproduce the published LRD–PSR comparisons directly (rather than relying on mosaics), this archive provides the calibrated inputs for custom polar projections, photometric normalization, and reflectance analysis."},{"path":"img/data/messenger/MDIS/MESSDEM_1001/","dataset_id":"MESS-H-MDIS-5-DEM-ELEVATION-V1.0","title":"MESSENGER MDIS DIGITAL ELEVATION MODEL ARCHIVE","pds_version":"PDS3","mission":"messenger","node":"img","reasoning":"Stage 1 (resolve + probe): While not an image mosaic, the MDIS DEM is commonly paired with MDIS imaging to model permanent shadow at the north pole (terrain-controlled illumination) and then compare predicted PSR extent with observed low-reflectance deposits in MDIS basemaps."}]}
